description: MITRE ATT&CK rules for disk forensics analysis. These rules analyze feature vectors extracted from TSK (The Sleuth Kit) output including file system metadata, extension distributions, temporal patterns, suspicious file indicators, inode statistics, and composite risk scores.

rules.description: Detects executable files located in temporary directories, a common indicator of malware staging. Threat actors frequently drop malicious executables in Temp directories because they are writable by default, rarely monitored, and provide a low-profile location for initial payload execution before establishing persistence.
rules.recommendation: Enumerate all executable files in temporary directories and compute file hashes for threat intelligence lookup. Check each executable's digital signature and verify against known-good software catalogs. Analyze file creation timestamps to establish a timeline of malware staging. Submit suspicious executables to a sandbox environment for dynamic analysis. Quarantine confirmed malicious files while preserving copies for evidence.

rules.description: Identifies executable files in the Downloads directory, which may indicate user-downloaded malware, social engineering payloads, or drive-by download artifacts. While some executables in Downloads are legitimate, a high count combined with other indicators suggests systematic malware delivery targeting end users.
rules.recommendation: List all executables in the Downloads directory with file hashes, sizes, and timestamps. Cross-reference with browser history to identify download sources. Verify digital signatures and check file hashes against VirusTotal and other threat intelligence databases. Analyze Zone.Identifier alternate data streams for URL origin information. Check for correlation with phishing email timestamps in the user's mailbox.

rules.description: Detects a high count of executable files on the Desktop, which may indicate malware payloads placed for easy user execution, social engineering artifacts, or attacker tools staged on the Desktop for convenient access during an active compromise.
rules.recommendation: Inventory all executable files on the Desktop and verify each against authorized software lists. Check for recently created executables with suspicious names or disguised icons. Analyze file metadata including embedded version information and digital signatures. Look for executables masquerading as documents or shortcuts. Review user activity logs to determine if the executables were deliberately placed or automatically created.

rules.description: Identifies files using double extensions (e.g., document.pdf.exe, invoice.docx.scr) which is a classic social engineering technique to disguise executable files as benign documents. Double extensions exploit the Windows default behavior of hiding known file extensions, making malicious executables appear to be safe document files.
rules.recommendation: List all files with double extensions and categorize by true file type. Prioritize files with executable final extensions (.exe, .scr, .bat, .cmd, .ps1, .vbs). Check file headers (magic bytes) to verify actual file type versus displayed extension. Hash all double-extension executables and query threat intelligence feeds. Investigate the directories containing these files and the timestamps of their creation. Alert users about the social engineering technique.

rules.description: Detects hidden executable files on the filesystem. While some system executables are legitimately hidden, hidden executables in user-accessible locations are a strong indicator of malware attempting to evade visual detection by file system explorers and basic security scans.
rules.mitre_technique_name: Hide Artifacts: Hidden Files and Directories
rules.recommendation: Enumerate all hidden executable files including their full paths, sizes, hashes, and timestamps. Verify each against known system files and legitimate software. Investigate hidden executables in non-system directories as high-priority indicators. Check for hidden directories that may contain attacker toolkits. Analyze file content and metadata for malware indicators. Remove the hidden attribute and quarantine confirmed malicious files.

rules.description: Identifies deleted executable files that may indicate anti-forensics activity where an attacker deleted tools after use, or malware that self-deletes to avoid detection. The presence of deleted executables in the file system journal or MFT records provides valuable forensic evidence of previously executed malicious programs.
rules.recommendation: Attempt to recover deleted executables using file carving tools and MFT analysis. Extract file names, paths, sizes, and timestamps from MFT or journal records. Compute hashes of recovered files for threat intelligence lookup. Correlate deletion timestamps with other suspicious activity in the timeline. Check for patterns of execution followed by deletion that indicate self-deleting malware. Preserve all recovered artifacts as forensic evidence.

rules.description: Detects a high count of persistence-related artifacts including startup entries, scheduled tasks, registry run keys, and service installations. An elevated persistence indicator count suggests that malware or an attacker has established multiple persistence mechanisms to survive system reboots and maintain access to the compromised system.
rules.recommendation: Analyze all identified persistence artifacts to determine which are legitimate and which are malicious. Inspect startup folders, registry run keys, scheduled tasks, services, and WMI subscriptions. Cross-reference persistence mechanisms with known malware families. Document each persistence artifact with its location, associated executable, and creation timestamp. Remove confirmed malicious persistence entries while preserving evidence. Monitor for re-establishment of persistence after removal.

rules.description: Identifies abuse of the Windows Startup folder by detecting elevated persistence indicators combined with a high count of LNK (shortcut) files. Dropping malicious shortcuts or executables in the Startup folder ensures automatic execution on every user logon, providing a simple but effective persistence mechanism frequently used by commodity malware.
rules.mitre_technique_name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
rules.recommendation: Inspect all LNK files in Startup folders for both current user and all-users profiles. Parse LNK file metadata to identify target executables, command-line arguments, and working directories. Check for LNK files pointing to executables in temporary or unusual directories. Verify each Startup entry against authorized software. Analyze LNK creation timestamps to identify when persistence was established. Remove unauthorized entries and monitor for re-creation.

rules.description: Detects massive file deletion activity indicated by a high deletion ratio and large number of deleted files. This pattern is consistent with anti-forensics operations, ransomware post-encryption cleanup, or insider threats systematically destroying data. Mass deletion significantly impacts forensic recovery capabilities.
rules.recommendation: Assess the extent of file deletion and categorize deleted files by type, directory, and deletion timestamp. Attempt data recovery using file carving and MFT/journal analysis before any write operations to the disk. Check for secure deletion patterns (overwritten data) versus simple deletion (recoverable). Correlate deletion activity with user sessions and process execution. Verify backup availability and integrity for affected data. Preserve the disk image for future recovery attempts.

rules.description: Identifies suspicious burst patterns in file creation activity. File creation bursts may indicate malware deployment, payload extraction, archive decompression attacks, or automated tool installation. Rapid file creation concentrated in short time windows is unusual for normal user activity and warrants investigation.
rules.recommendation: Analyze the timing and distribution of file creation bursts to identify distinct episodes. For each burst, examine the file types, directories, and naming patterns of created files. Check for correlation with malware execution timestamps. Investigate whether bursts correspond to legitimate software installations or automated processes. Look for encrypted file extensions that may indicate ransomware activity. Document burst patterns for timeline reconstruction.

rules.description: Detects an unusually high number of file modification bursts. Burst modification patterns can indicate ransomware encrypting files in rapid succession, data manipulation attacks, or anti-forensics timestamp manipulation. The concentration of modifications in short time windows distinguishes malicious activity from normal file system operations.
rules.recommendation: Analyze file modification burst patterns to identify the specific time windows and affected directories. Check for file extension changes that may indicate ransomware encryption. Compare modified file headers against their extensions to detect content alteration. Look for ransom notes or attacker communications created during modification bursts. Verify whether modification patterns correspond to legitimate batch operations or software updates.

rules.description: Detects potential timestamp manipulation (timestomping) by identifying high standard deviation in timestamp gaps combined with files lacking timestamps. Timestamp manipulation is a defense evasion technique used to make malicious files appear to have been created during the original OS installation or to disrupt forensic timeline analysis.
rules.recommendation: Identify files with anomalous timestamps including dates far in the future or past. Compare $STANDARD_INFORMATION timestamps against $FILE_NAME timestamps in MFT records to detect timestomping (they should be similar for legitimate files). Analyze timestamp gap distributions to identify clusters of manipulated files. Cross-reference suspicious timestamps with known malware deployment techniques. Use journal analysis to recover original timestamps where possible.

rules.description: Identifies an abnormally high ratio of deleted inodes to total inodes, indicating systematic file deletion across the filesystem. A high deleted inode ratio suggests that a significant portion of files have been removed, which may indicate anti-forensics activity, data wiping, or post-incident cleanup by an attacker.
rules.recommendation: Analyze deleted inode metadata to reconstruct what was deleted and when. Attempt to recover file names and partial content from directory entries and journal records. Map deletion patterns across the filesystem to identify targeted directories. Check for selective deletion of specific file types (logs, executables, documents). Verify whether deleted files can be recovered through file carving of unallocated space.

rules.description: Detects scripts located in suspicious directories combined with an elevated total script count. Scripts in directories like Temp, AppData, or ProgramData may indicate fileless malware delivery, attacker post-exploitation tools, or automated malicious activity using PowerShell, batch, VBScript, or JavaScript.
rules.recommendation: List all script files in suspicious directories with full paths, sizes, and timestamps. Analyze script content for obfuscation, encoded payloads, download cradles, or system reconnaissance commands. Check for scripts that spawn additional processes, modify registry, or establish network connections. Verify scripts against known legitimate automation and system management tools. Quarantine confirmed malicious scripts and trace their origin.

rules.description: Identifies an unusually high number of executable files with an elevated executable-to-total-file ratio. Executable proliferation may indicate widespread malware infection with self-replicating behavior, worm propagation across network shares, or deployment of a large attacker toolkit across the filesystem.
rules.recommendation: Analyze the distribution of executable files across the filesystem to identify unusual concentrations. Hash all executables and compare against known-good software inventories. Identify executables with similar sizes, creation dates, or naming patterns that may indicate replication. Check for known worm signatures and self-propagation mechanisms. Verify executable locations against standard software installation paths.

rules.description: Detects a combination of executables in temporary directories with elevated temporary file counts, strongly suggesting malware staging in temp folders. This is a more specific indicator than general temp executables, as the combination with high temp file counts suggests active malware that is creating working files alongside its payload.
rules.recommendation: Prioritize investigation of executables in Temp directories. Analyze associated temp files for malware working data, configuration files, or encrypted payloads. Check for parent-child relationships between temp executables and other processes. Monitor for new file creation in Temp directories indicating ongoing malicious activity. Hash and submit all temp executables for threat intelligence analysis. Clean confirmed malware from temp directories after evidence preservation.

rules.description: Detects potential archive bomb (zip bomb) activity by identifying an unusually high archive file count combined with file creation bursts. Archive bombs are malicious compressed files that expand to an enormous size when extracted, designed to crash or overwhelm disk space, antivirus scanners, or forensic analysis tools.
rules.recommendation: Identify all archive files and analyze their compression ratios (high compression ratios may indicate zip bombs). Do NOT extract suspicious archives on production systems — use isolated sandboxes. Check for nested archives (archives within archives) that are a hallmark of zip bomb techniques. Verify archive contents against expected data formats. Analyze the relationship between archive files and creation bursts to determine if bursts resulted from archive extraction.

rules.description: Detects abnormally high filesystem activity during nighttime and weekend hours. While some systems operate around the clock, elevated ratios of night and weekend activity on user workstations are a strong indicator of automated malicious activity, scheduled task abuse, or attacker operations timed to avoid detection during normal business hours.
rules.recommendation: Analyze the types of file operations occurring during off-hours periods. Identify processes and user accounts active during nighttime and weekend hours. Check for scheduled tasks or cron jobs that may be responsible for the off-hours activity. Determine if the system is a server (where off-hours activity may be normal) or a user workstation (where it is suspicious). Correlate off-hours activity with other security events to identify coordinated attack operations.
